title: Understanding the Uses of Leysa and Inna in Sentences
summary: The class reviewed key concepts from Tamrin 8, focusing on the correct usage of 'leysa' with different grammatical structures and the emphasis added by 'inna'. Students practiced forming responses using the same words as questions and explored exceptions to grammatical rules introduced by these particles.
key_points: Leysa must be paired with a word showing negation and affects subsequent words: لَيْسَ أَبِي بِمُهَنْدِسٍ (Laysa abiy bi-muhandis).
Ibnu takes the haraka of the preceding word and is mudaf to the following word, as in Khalidu ibn Walidin.
In sentences with 'leysa', ensure the response uses the same pronoun form as the question (e.g., answering "Is your father a engineer?" with مهندس).
The particle 'inna' emphasizes and affects the following mubtada, giving it fatha: إِنَّ فِي جَيْبِ مِئَتَ رِيَالٍ (Inna fi jaybim aidat riyal).
Numbers like 'meyat' (100) are treated as musdafa ilaihi, requiring singular form: مِئَتَ رِيَالٍ.
Harf al-jar (e.g., fi, bi) does not receive the fatha from 'inna', only the mubtada does.
questions: [00:02:31,789] Q: What is the rule for Ibnu? — A: It takes the haraka of the previous word and affects the next (Khalidu ibn Walidin).
[00:06:00,079] Q: Why is 'tanwin' removed when using leysa? — A: Leysa negates the state, so 'leyset' for third person female singular.
[00:12:00,080] Q: How to identify mubtada and khabar in complex sentences? — A: Mubtada is ma'ruf (with alif lam or proper noun), khabar is nakir (new information).
[00:24:00,160] Q: Why does 'amutazawij' use fatha? — A: After 'inna', mubtada receives fatha: إِنَّكَ مُتَزْوِجاً (Innaka mutazawjan).
